topic: Someone discovers their partner has been writing a novel about them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crumpled manuscript lay on the coffee table, a thick sheaf of pages filled with my own life, my ridiculous habits, my deepest, most embarrassing secrets, all meticulously woven into a sprawling narrative. I stared at it, my jaw tight, the muscles in my neck corded with tension. When Liam finally walked in, whistling some cheerful tune, I didn't look up. I just pointed a rigid finger at the stack of paper. "What is this?" My voice was low, dangerously flat. He froze, the whistling dying in his throat. The sudden silence in the apartment felt heavy, suffocating. "It's mine," I managed, the words tasting like ash. His face slowly shifted, a flicker of something—surprise mixed with a hesitant defensiveness—crossing his features. I stood up, the scrape of my chair against the hardwood floor sounding unnervingly loud. "You wrote this about me?" The accusation hung in the air, sharp and undeniable.

[story 2]

I found the evidence tucked beneath a stack of utility bills—a manuscript, bound in worn leather, clearly a novel. My hands trembled as I picked it up, the paper feeling brittle under my fingertips. A slow, hot flush spread across my chest, making my breath catch. I hadn't seen it; I hadn't realized it was there until now. When Mark arrived for dinner, I slammed the book onto the counter, the sound echoing sharply in the quiet kitchen. "Explain this," I demanded, my voice pitched higher than I intended. He stopped mid-stride, his expression morphing into one of startled confusion. "What are you talking about?" He looked genuinely bewildered, running a hand through his already messy hair. I crossed my arms tightly across my chest, refusing to soften the line of my posture. "Don't play dumb with me, Mark. This is… personal. You've been writing a book about my existence?" The air crackled between us, thick with unspoken frustration.

[story 3]

The discovery hit me like a physical blow. I was in the study, ostensibly looking for a misplaced book, and there it was: a manuscript detailing every argument, every quiet triumph, every awkward glance I had ever experienced. A deep, churning resentment started in my gut, rising quickly into a hot, prickly sensation that made my palms sweat. When Sarah walked in, humming softly, I didn't bother trying to hide the book. I held it up, my knuckles white against the cover. "Are you kidding me?" I spat out, the words clipped and sharp. Sarah stopped dead, her smile vanishing instantly, replaced by a look of profound shock. "What is that?" she asked, her tone cautious. I took a step toward her, my body language radiating a rigid refusal to let this stand. "This is *my* life, Sarah. And you've been turning it into some kind of… project?" My voice was strained, vibrating with suppressed anger. "I need you to tell me why you thought you had the right to write about me."
